---
title: "Lab 1 — Install the stack"
subtitle: "R, RStudio, TinyTeX, Quarto (and optional Git)"
toc: true
---

This lab gets every required tool installed and **rendering one
Quarto document end-to-end**. It is the practical companion to
[Week 1 — Your first render](../notes/week01-first-render.qmd), which
is the short conceptual reading for Week 1.

Work through this top to bottom. If you get stuck on a step, come to
one of the scheduled studio meetings in the MAC or email me.

## What you'll have at the end

- **R** (the language) installed.
- **RStudio** (the editor) installed.
- **TinyTeX** (a minimal LaTeX distribution) installed via R.
- **Quarto** installed and runnable from the command line.
- **(Optional) Git** installed and usable.
- A **`math-software-portfolio/`** folder somewhere sensible on your
  computer.
- A `.qmd` file that renders to both HTML and PDF without errors.

If you already have some of these — great, skip ahead.

## 0. Pick a portfolio location

Make a folder you will not move or rename for the semester. Suggested:

- macOS / Linux: `~/Documents/math-software-portfolio/`
- Windows: `C:\Users\YourName\Documents\math-software-portfolio\`

Inside that folder, create a `hw01/` subfolder. You'll put your first
`.qmd` there.

## 1. Install R

Download R from <https://cran.r-project.org> and install with all
defaults.

- macOS — pick the `.pkg` file matching your chip (Apple Silicon or
  Intel).
- Windows — pick the `.exe` installer.
- Linux — use your package manager (`apt`, `dnf`, etc.) or follow CRAN's
  instructions for your distro.

**Verify:** open the system terminal (Terminal on mac/Linux, PowerShell
on Windows) and run:

```bash
R --version
```

You should see a version string. If "command not found," restart your
terminal and try again; if it still fails, R is not on your PATH —
flag this in the MAC.

## 2. Install RStudio

Download RStudio Desktop (Open Source) from
<https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/>. Default install.

**Verify:** open RStudio. The four-pane layout should appear. In the
Console pane, type `1 + 1` and press Enter — you should see `[1] 2`.

## 3. Install TinyTeX

TinyTeX is a small, install-on-demand LaTeX distribution. We use it
because it's much smaller than full TeX Live and works well with
Quarto. **Do this from inside R (or RStudio's Console):**

```r
install.packages("tinytex")
tinytex::install_tinytex()
```

The install can take a few minutes. If `install_tinytex()` finishes
without errors, you're done.

**Verify:**

```r
tinytex::tinytex_root()
```

This should print a path like `/Users/you/Library/TinyTeX` or
`C:\Users\You\AppData\Roaming\TinyTeX`.

## 4. Install Quarto

Download Quarto from <https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/>. Default
install.

**Verify:** in your system terminal:

```bash
quarto --version
```

You should see a version string like `1.x.y`.

## 5. (Optional) Install Git

Git is **optional** for this course. You only need it if you plan to
make your portfolio a public or private GitHub repository. A local
folder works fine for assignment submission.

- macOS — `git` ships with Xcode command-line tools. Run
  `xcode-select --install` if not already done.
- Windows — install Git for Windows from <https://git-scm.com/download/win>.
- Linux — `sudo apt install git` (or your distro's equivalent).

**Verify:** `git --version` in your terminal.

If you choose to use a GitHub repo for your portfolio, see the
optional "first portfolio repo" lab later in the term.

## 6. Render your first Quarto document

In your `hw01/` folder, create a file called `hw01.qmd` with these
contents:

````markdown
---
title: "Hello, Quarto"
author: "YOUR NAME"
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
---

# A first equation

The quadratic formula:

$$
x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}.
$$

# A first R chunk

```{{r}}
set.seed(1)
x <- rnorm(10)
mean(x)
```

# AI Use Note

**Tool:** [assistant name and approximate version, or "did not use AI"]
**Purpose:** [what you used it for, if any]
**Verification:** [how you checked the output, if any]
````

You have two ways to render the file:

**Easiest — RStudio.** Open `hw01.qmd` in RStudio. Click the
**Render** button at the top of the editor pane. The PDF preview
opens automatically when the render succeeds.

**Always works — system terminal.**

```bash
cd path/to/math-software-portfolio/hw01
quarto render hw01.qmd
```

Either way, you should see `hw01.html` and `hw01.pdf` next to your
`.qmd`.

## Common problems

- **PDF fails to compile.** Run `tinytex::install_tinytex()` in R again
  to make sure TinyTeX completed. If LaTeX complains about a missing
  package, run `quarto render hw01.qmd` a second time — TinyTeX will
  download missing packages on demand the first time.
- **`quarto: command not found`.** Quarto isn't on your PATH. Quit and
  reopen the terminal. If that doesn't work, the installer didn't
  finish — reinstall and watch for permission prompts.
- **R chunk doesn't run.** Make sure you have the language fence
  written exactly as ```` ```{r} ```` (lowercase `r`, no extra spaces
  inside the braces).
- **Equation doesn't render.** Math goes between `$ ... $` (inline) or
  `$$ ... $$` (display). No backslashes before the dollar signs.

## What this prepares you to do

When you finish this lab you should be able to:

- run R, RStudio, TinyTeX, and Quarto on your own computer,
- find your **`math-software-portfolio/`** folder and a `hw01/`
  subfolder inside it,
- edit a `.qmd` source file,
- render that source to both HTML and PDF without errors,
- locate the rendered files next to the source.

The Week 1 assignment in the course LMS uses exactly this workflow —
the course LMS holds the prompt, the file-naming convention, and the
submission area. The **Week 1 Setup conference** is where we confirm
together that the chain works end-to-end; be ready to show your
rendered document, your source file, and your portfolio folder when
we meet.
